nativeRange.habitat: Well-protected reefs of lagoons and bays; typically inshore at depths of around 6–50 m, where it tends to occur in mixed-sex groups above coral patches and rubble.

decorPreferences: live rock with multiple caves and overhangs
open swimming space in the mid-water column

diet.notes: Wild diet is principally zooplankton drifting above reef structure. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, copepods, amphipods, and high-quality prepared foods. Generally advised to feed small portions 2–3 times per day to support a high metabolism; a refugium seeding live copepods and amphipods is commonly considered beneficial.

goodWith: other peaceful to semi-aggressive reef fish
avoidWith: additional male Stocky Anthias in modest-sized systems

summary: An Indo-Pacific anthias with a stockier, deeper body than its relatives; generally considered one of the more manageable Pseudanthias, but still benefits from a harem of one male with several females in a well-fed, covered reef system.
careNotes:
Pseudanthias hypselosoma is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite — individuals are born female and the dominant fish in a group will transition to male in the absence of one. If the dominant male is lost, the largest female has been reported to morph to replace it. Males generally show more intense coloration (orange/pink with yellow markings) during displays, while females tend to be plainer pink.

Generally best kept as a single male with several females (harem). Multiple males in modest-sized tanks tend to fight; a sufficiently large system with abundant rockwork can dilute conspecific aggression. Single individuals can also do well.

Anthias have a high metabolism and have been reported to decline quickly if underfed. Multiple small feedings per day are commonly advised, and a refugium producing live copepods and amphipods is widely recommended.

Like most anthias, this species has been documented jumping from open aquaria; a tight-fitting lid or full cover is generally advised.

Cross-source figures: LiveAquaria states ~17.8 cm (7 in) max; FishBase lists max 19.0 cm TL with common length ~15 cm SL. Adult range here (12–18 cm) reflects typical captive adults with males larger than females. Temperature: LiveAquaria 22–26°C, FishBase preferred 22–27°C — averaged to 22–26°C. Minimum tank size: LiveAquaria and FishyWiki both recommend 125 gal (~472 L); rounded up to 500 L here to account for the species' active mid-water swimming, harem-keeping recommendations, and jumping/escape risk that benefit from a larger footprint.

Note on family classification: LiveAquaria lists the species under Serranidae (the traditional grouping), while FishBase places it in Anthiadidae following the recent split. Anthiadidae is used here as the current accepted family.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is generally considered difficult. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, sex change occurs socially when the dominant male is absent. Broadcast spawning has been observed in larger aquarium systems, but rearing the small planktivorous larvae through metamorphosis is rarely achieved in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care sheet: 125 gal minimum, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, max ~7 in, carnivore, semi-aggressive, reef safe, hermaphroditic, best with own species.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Family Anthiadidae, Order Perciformes), max size (19.0 cm TL, common 15 cm SL), preferred temperature 22–27°C, depth range 6–50 m, and Indo-Pacific distribution (Maldives to Samoa, Taiwan to Great Barrier Reef).

additional.name: FishyWiki
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (125 gal / 472 L), adult size (17.8 cm), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, KH 8–12, semi-aggressive, reef-compatible, carnivorous diet.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite; sex change occurs socially when the dominant male is absent. Broadcast spawning has been observed in larger systems but larval rearing is rarely achieved in home aquariums.
